input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware architecture comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception and control modules). The vehicle operates in a test‑track environment where it can be driven remotely and is used to evaluate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functions. The scale model and modular chassis make it suitable for digital twinning because it provides a controllable, safety‑critical platform that can generate rich vehicle‑state data for off‑board processing and remote interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; it also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of historical data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed computation, and desired‑speed output) is executed on cloud resources rather than on the resource‑constrained on‑board ECU, with the computed speed transmitted back to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable or disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency braking command via the UI. The services support low‑latency bi‑directional communication, data collection, and safety‑critical command execution, and are used by two user classes: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between devices with a maximum error of 100 ms; this bound is imposed because hardware can only support 100 ms accuracy, whereas safety‑critical vehicular standards often demand tighter sync (<100 ms). The synchronization interval is therefore set to maintain ≤100 ms offset, enabling computation off‑loading between the physical twin and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference model, instantiated as three top‑level entities—User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and Physical Twin Entity. Each entity hosts one or more Functional Entities (FEs) that are implemented as ROS 2 packages on separate hardware components, yielding a decentralized deployment rather than a single centralized twin. No evidence of multiple parallel twin instances is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: functional entities are mapped to ROS 2 packages, each package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE functionality. The three entities are deployed on distinct compute nodes (e.g., vehicle ECU, sensor processor) and communicate via ROS 2 topics/services, providing system‑wide coordination of data collection, actuation, and user interaction. This component‑based integration implements the Digital Twin Entity for data management, the Physical Twin Entity for sensor/actuator handling, and the User Entity for 3D visualization and control panel.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Within the Digital Twin Entity, data management is provided by a dedicated Data Storage functional entity. Computational models are realized as ROS 2 nodes packaged per functional entity; the primary model implemented is a point‑cloud clustering and tracking algorithm that supports the 3‑D visualization component. Model inputs originate from the sensors attached to the Physical Twin Entity, and model outputs are consumed by the User Interface FE. All models and data components are organized as ROS 2 packages mapped to ISO 23247 functional entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality resides in the Physical Twin Entity. Functional entities responsible for actuation are implemented as ROS 2 packages containing nodes that issue control commands to vehicle actuators (e.g., motor and steering controllers) via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. Control commands are exposed to the User Interface FE through the Control Panel component, allowing remote operation of the OME. No specific actuator types or ranges are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure is represented by the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) and its associated sensors, which feed raw measurements into the Physical Twin Entity. The Data Collection and Device Control Entity gathers these sensor streams and forwards them to ROS 2 nodes for preprocessing (e.g., point‑cloud generation). Specific sensor modalities, sampling rates, or accuracy specifications are not enumerated in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system requires a shared notion of time across devices with a synchronization accuracy of 100 ms, as mandated by hardware limitations and safety‑critical vehicular application constraints. This timing requirement underpins the validity of actuation and data fusion in the Digital Twin. No additional model validation, uncertainty quantification, or quality assurance procedures are described in the source materials.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point cloud, motor feedback, steering servo status, vehicle state) are collected by the Data Collecting FE, passed to the Data Translation FE which serializes the streams, performs unit conversion and aligns message types, then transmitted over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP (transport layer) to the Digital Twin System. Within the DTS the data are exchanged between software components on a ROS 2 middleware bus. Before visualisation, raw LiDAR points are pre‑processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component (Data Pre‑Processing FE) to generate object tracks. Transmission is event‑driven by sensor updates; no explicit frequency is given. Data quality assurance is performed by the preprocessing step and by the Data Translation component.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the ACC Controlling FE (e.g., desired speed, enable/disable ACC, emergency braking) are packaged by the Data Translation FE, transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link to the Physical Twin Entity, and received by the Actuation FE which drives the motor, steering and servo actuators. The command messages follow the format defined by the Data Translation component (aligned with ISO 23247). Validation occurs within the Data Translation and Actuation FE before actuation; feedback of execution status is sent back through the same channel to the DTS.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS employs a two‑layer communication architecture: (1) an internal ROS 2 (DDS) network for all software component interactions, and (2) an external Wi‑Fi network using UDP for bi‑directional data exchange between the Physical Twin and the Digital Twin. The system is required (Requirement R7) to maintain end‑to‑end network latency of at most 100 ms, and (Requirement R8) to provide clock synchronization between the twin entities (e.g., via NTP or ROS 2 time sync). Reliability is supported by ROS 2’s QoS policies, while security mechanisms are not explicitly detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is provided through the User Interface Functional Entity, which is split into a 3D Visualization component and a Control Panel component. These components enable users to monitor real‑time data from the Digital Twin Entity and to issue control commands to the Physical Twin Entity (i.e., the Observable Manufacturing Element). Interaction is limited to manual monitoring and control; the documents do not describe automated analytics, insight generation algorithms, or confidence metrics for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The architecture explicitly follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, adopting its entity‑based model (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and mapping software components to Functional Entities. Implementation of Functional Entities is realized as ROS 2 packages, each containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The documents note that OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) can be used for communication, reflecting adherence to industrial interoperability standards. Additionally, timing requirements reference vehicular safety standards that mandate clock synchronization accuracy better than 100 ms, indicating compliance with relevant time‑synchronization specifications.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle, its retrofit from a teleoperated base, and the chassis layers (lower and upper). It correctly identifies the use for ACC evaluation. However, it generalizes specific hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Hokuyo LiDAR) as 'advanced sensors and control technology' and mentions a 'test-track environment' which is not explicitly stated in the text (the text refers to a 'real-world setting' or 'case study'). Quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'The physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis', 'retrofitting it with autonomy components'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the three core services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) as described in Section III.A. It accurately captures the capabilities of the UI, the specific logic offloaded to the cloud/DT (ACC calculation), and the remote control commands. It also correctly identifies the user classes (operators and developers) and the requirement for low latency. Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', '3) Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly list several tools and enablers. Section III.B mentions 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0' as the SDK, 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)' as middleware, and programming languages 'Python 3.8', 'C++', and 'MATLAB/Simulink'. Section IV.B describes mapping software components onto FEs using 'ROS2 package'. Section IV.C refers to the 'Data Translation component' corresponding to ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. Since the information is present, the 'Not in Document' claim is incorrect. Supported by quotes: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)... and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8..., C++..., and MATLAB/Simulink', and 'All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE'.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the source text regarding clock synchronization and latency. The text states: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and explains the bound is due to 'hardware limitations' while acknowledging stricter standards exist. It also specifies the latency requirement: 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity' to support 'computation offloading'. The extraction is precise and grounded in the text.

The description correctly details the ISO 23247-based instantiation of three entities: User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin. It accurately notes that Functional Entities (FEs) are implemented as ROS 2 packages and deployed on 'different hardware component[s]', resulting in a decentralized architecture. It correctly states no evidence exists for multiple parallel twin instances. Supported by quotes: 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity', 'SW components that implement data management... are organized into the Digital Twin Entity', and 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The description accurately captures the system orchestration: FEs mapped to ROS 2 packages, deployed on distinct compute nodes (e.g., 'vehicle ECU' implied by hardware descriptions), and communicating via ROS 2. It correctly identifies the roles of the User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin entities as described in the text (e.g., User Entity for '3D Visualization and control panel'). Supported by quotes: 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and the descriptions of entities in Section IV-B.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses entirely on the internal architecture of the Adaptive Cruise Control Digital Twin, its ISO 23247 mapping, ROS 2 implementation, and specific services (monitoring, offloading, remote control). It does not discuss integration with external systems, other digital twins, or enterprise-level interoperability standards beyond the internal ROS 2 and ISO 23247 references. Therefore, the extraction is correct.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data management via a dedicated Data Storage functional entity and the computational models realized as ROS 2 nodes. It correctly identifies the primary model (point-cloud clustering and tracking), its role in 3D visualization, input sources from the Physical Twin Entity, and consumption by the User Interface FE. It also notes the organization as ROS 2 packages mapped to ISO 23247 FEs. The text supports these details: 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database' (Table I), 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter' (Table I), and 'To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' (Section IV-B). The score is 4 because while correct and specific, it could be expanded slightly with more depth on the specific data flows or relationships mentioned in the architecture section.

The extraction correctly locates actuation in the Physical Twin Entity and describes implementation as ROS 2 packages issuing control commands via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. It accurately identifies the actuators (motor and steering controllers) and the exposure of commands through the Control Panel for remote operation. The text confirms: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I) and 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands' (Table I). The statement 'No specific actuator types or ranges are detailed in the documents' is consistent with the provided text, which focuses on high-level architecture rather than detailed hardware specs. The score is 5 because the extraction is highly specific, grounded in the sources, and accurately captures the scope of information available regarding physical actuation.

The extraction correctly identifies the sensing infrastructure as the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) and associated sensors feeding into the Physical Twin Entity. It notes the role of the Data Collection and Device Control Entity in gathering streams and forwarding them to ROS 2 nodes for preprocessing, including point-cloud generation. The text supports this: 'The Data Collection and Device Control Entity is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the OME' (Section II-A) and 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' (Section III-B). The extraction accurately notes that specific sampling rates or accuracy specs are not in the documents. The score is 4 because it is correct and specific, though it could be slightly more detailed regarding the specific sensor types listed in the source.

The extraction accurately identifies the clock synchronization requirement of 100 ms, mandated by hardware limitations and safety-critical constraints, as a key validity consideration. The text states: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations' (Section IV-A). The extraction correctly links this to actuation and data fusion validity. It also accurately notes that no additional model validation or uncertainty quantification is described. The score is 4 because it captures the specific technical detail available in the text regarding timing, though the concept of 'fidelity' in the broader sense (e.g., model accuracy) is not deeply explored beyond timing.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
